title: test-race: podman pod rm -a doesn't remove a running container
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

Test occasionally in CI fails, when running as a user (rootless testing).  

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Re-run tests until it fails :frowning_face: 

**Describe the results you received:**

``Error: pod 15d...74f contains containers and cannot be removed: container already exists``

**Describe the results you expected:**

Test consistently passes

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Code is: ``test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go`` line 97

**Output of `podman version`:**

Happening on master, ``v1.1.2-269-gb83d0572``

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Two examples:

* https://cirrus-ci.com/build/6328612444700672
* https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5367713881915392

comments.body:
That is expected.
```
man podman pod rm 
...
  -f, --force    Force removal of a running pod by first stopping all containers, then removing all containers in the pod.  The default is false
```

Correction: It's line 119 that fails.

@rhatdan That's what the test appears to be exercising: Confirm that rm -a fails.  This part is passing, it's the final check that fails, and only when running as a user (works fine when run as root):

```golang
    ...cut...
        // one pod should have been deleted
        result = podmanTest.Podman([]string{"pod", "ps", "-q"})
        result.WaitWithDefaultTimeout()
        Expect(len(result.OutputToStringArray())).To(Equal(1))
```

*sigh* so this rings some bells...it fails whenever there is NOT exactly one line of output :cry:

This is the worst setup to debug since we have NO idea what the multiple lines of output contain :confounded:   Maybe the first step in fixing this is to add some helpful output...

Ha! I think I caught it.  This is the ps output when the test is expecting ***1*** pod:

```POD ID         NAME              STATUS    CREATED        # OF CONTAINERS   INFRA ID
e67b1480769c   cool_jones        Created   1 second ago   0                 
d78c9ca2baf9   elastic_hermann   Running   1 second ago   1
```

So...since we're running tests in parallel, I think maybe this is another one for  #2649 ?
This can't be  #2649 because separate ``--root and --runroot`` guarantee separation.  Therefor the unexpected pod must be coming from somewhere else.

So on my initial look, this test seems to make two pods, only one running, and then do an `rm -a` to remove only the not-running pod.

Initial diagnosis: the not-running pod is either not being deleted at all (really bad) or we're racing and it's still there when we do a `ps` (more likely, not as bad)

@mheon That's helpful, thanks.  I believe this is a race, sometimes it passes.  Sounds like the rm -a is exiting before the pod is actually removed.  Okay, I'll see if I can reproduce that manually (as a user).

@mheon Okay I'm with you now, having run the commands manually.  I agree your second theory seems more probable.  [I used a ``defer()`` to produce the output above](https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/2737/files#diff-763d32052d18ee5114bb2af368392a2bR109).  So I think that means there really were two pods still existing after the 'rm -a' happened (at least briefly).

Can you confirm that the 'rm -a' *can* return before all removals are complete?  If so, then all we need here is a retry/timeout loop on the final check.  I may even be able to manage writing one (or at least copying one) :grinning:
I don't think that should be possible... Part of `rm` that always happens before return is wiping the pod(s) in question from the database, so we should never see them again.
@mheon dang, well, it's def. happening or my eyeballs are lying to me.  I'll double-check, but I'm pretty sure the `defer()` I added ran a `podman pod ps -a` AFTER the test failed (count 2)...

...Yep, sure'nuff, from [the run with the `defer(podman pod ps -a)` the failure came from line 126](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5760077825835008) (the final check).  Here's the full output of the test:

```

Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test083479571 --storage-driver vfs pod ps -q
e67b1480769c
d78c9ca2baf9
output: e67b1480769c d78c9ca2baf9
Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test083479571 --storage-driver vfs pod ps
POD ID         NAME              STATUS    CREATED        # OF CONTAINERS   INFRA ID
e67b1480769c   cool_jones        Created   1 second ago   0                 
d78c9ca2baf9   elastic_hermann   Running   1 second ago   1                 
output: POD ID NAME STATUS CREATED # OF CONTAINERS INFRA ID e67b1480769c cool_jones Created 1 second ago 0 d78c9ca2baf9 elastic_hermann Running 1 second ago 1
podman pod ps: 
POD ID NAME STATUS CREATED # OF CONTAINERS INFRA ID e67b1480769c cool_jones Created 1 second ago 0 d78c9ca2baf9 elastic_hermann Running 1 second ago 1
[AfterEach] Podman pod rm
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:31
Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test083479571/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test083479571 --storage-driver vfs pod rm -fa
d78c9ca2baf993c0ed3924c6c04c8f3d18479a3c4f34d41f2dfa25ed83f29237
e67b1480769c431af35354e9be331df95ecdfd6b55a077d16f0abab384a21b2f
• Failure [4.302 seconds]
Podman pod rm
/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:14
  podman pod rm -a doesn't remove a running container [It]
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:98
  Expected
      <int>: 2
  to equal
      <int>: 1
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:126
  Full Stack Trace
```

There was def. a "created" pod and a "running" pod :frowning_face: 

I'll attempt to add a retry/timeout loop...
So we still have 2 pods here, after `rm` finished. Alright, that's properly wierd.

@mheon yeah, and I'm assuming this test owns both of them. i.e. we're NOT seeing a pod from another, parallel test, or a leftover from prior test.  I'm also assuming my understanding of the output is correct (i.e. it may not be) :grin:
It looks like they're the same pods the test is trying to remove, so that sounds accurate

@mheon [Eww, a retry-loop doesn't fix this either](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4834354567053312) - also the ginkgo test fails...but somehow gets marked as a pass??? (ignoring this for now).  Remember: This is the rootless test (running this as root works fine).  What other data can I grab that would be helpful debugging this?

Maybe @giuseppe do you have any ideas here, how a user's ``podman pod rm -a`` would _sometimes_ return before removing an empty pod?

```
...cut...
Retry 10, pods 2Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test430270252 --storage-driver vfs pod ps -q
da42f5236eb8
73f729419d1c
output: da42f5236eb8 73f729419d1c
...cut...
Retry 1, pods 2Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test430270252 --storage-driver vfs pod ps -q
da42f5236eb8
73f729419d1c
output: da42f5236eb8 73f729419d1c
[AfterEach] Podman pod rm
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:32
Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test430270252/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test430270252 --storage-driver vfs pod rm -fa
73f729419d1ce60e6a53f4f0d772022cdb6a9ac0de84e8a87df77937b1a06798
da42f5236eb8e7ce302d6a827b29bf5ddb80c350f3c0c4c2a5e0d3b06569ff66
• Failure [60.155 seconds]
Podman pod rm
/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:15
  podman pod rm -a doesn't remove a running container [It]
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:99
  Expected
      <int>: 2
  to equal
      <int>: 1
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:130
  Full Stack Trace
```

OOOOOHHH! [I see how the test is passing](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5911203162357760):

```
SUCCESS! -- 1 Passed | 0 Failed | 1 Flaked | 0 Pending | 632 Skipped
```

Ginkgo is retrying it, so maybe something about the setup or cleanup is causing the problem then fixing it?

@mheon @baude Okay, [adding this line at the beginning of the test](https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/2737/files#diff-763d32052d18ee5114bb2af368392a2bR100) appears to help.  I need to do a few more re-runs to be sure though.  So, to recap this seems to be the behavior:

1. As root, all tests pass normally.
2. As a user, [this line ***SOMETIMES*** fails.](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go#L119)
3. When it fails, ``podman pod rm -a`` exits 0, but ``podman pod ps`` always shows the same two pods that were started
4. Adding the timeout/retry loop doesn't alter the behavior, ``ps`` shows the empty pod never gets removed.
5. Ginkgo attempts to to re-run it up to 3 more times.   Presumably [calling cleanup after each.](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go#L31).
6. Sometimes this works, sometimes not.
7. Adding a call at the start of the test to ``podmanTest.CleanupPod()`` appears to help, though one run isn't enough to be sure.

**Questions:**
1. How is it possible to have a race with ``podman pod rm -a`` which would ***SOMETIMES*** not remove empty pods but still exit 0.
2. Assuming step 7 (above) pattern persists, does that suggest ``CleanupPod()`` is removing or resetting some state which is shared, and should not be?
3. Is it possible multiple tests are using the same ``/tmp/blah`` directory?
4. What other resources could be mistakenly shared across multiple tests, that could explain this behavior?
**update:** Ugg, nope, step 7 doesn't affect behavior.  It just failed on the first run w/ ``CleanupPod()`` at the start.  So it's still demonstrating Step 3 (above) randomly :confounded:

@mheon is there any way I can instrument the test to give us more info on what's happening?  e.g. if the test detects this condition, how can I confirm the database matches reality (the removed pod exists or doesn't).?
@cevich I think you might want to try debug logging on `podman pod rm -a` first - see if it actually sees the pods and tries to remove them

@mheon dangit...shoulda seen this coming:  Adding debug seems to prevent the race:   As many races are...timing-dependent :face_with_head_bandage:    So, we'll have to do this somewhat blind, but maybe not completely.  The condition does seem to persist once it occurs.  I s'pose we don't yet know if this is a problem with the `rm` or with the `podman pod ps`.  Let me try just removing the `--log-level=debug` from the `rm` and see if it reproduces with only having it on the `ps`.

[Confirmed in this run](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4921309183082496) the problem reproduces without the debug on the ``podman pod rm -a``  and with it on the ``podman pod ps``.  @mheon I don't see anything incriminating in the output from the two failed run (a flake re-run was successful).

Since debugging the rm masks the problem, is there some easy-ish way to verify the directory structure and/or database contents are correct?  Basically, make sure this is a 'pod rm' problem and not a 'pod ps' problem.
If it still shows up in `ps` and `rm` did not error I think it's safe to assume that `rm` never fired on the pod in question - if we had DB issues subsequent commands would be blowing up.
If we throw a, say, 1 second sleep before the `rm -a`, does it resolve the race? I'm thinking we have some sort of race where `rm` doesn't see the pods and doesn't try to remove them

Ahh yes, we can see that in the logs:

```
  /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/test/e2e/pod_rm_test.go:99
Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test703242098/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test703242098/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test703242098 --storage-driver vfs pod rm -fa
Running: /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman --root /tmp/podman_test703242098/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test703242098/crio-run --runtime /usr/bin/runc --conmon /usr/libexec/podman/conmon --cni-config-dir /etc/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager cgroupfs --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test703242098 --storage-driver vfs --log-level=debug pod ps -q
```

It does NOT show the expected normal `Error: pod 783ea2e51f5da239b65201d5aacf0bf29bb6a29bcc486f804438c3fcde067554 contains containers and cannot be removed: container already exists`

In fact, that's something the test can look for (also).  Thanks @mheon I'll try adding the delay.

@mheon okay, 2-out-of-3 re-runs passed, [the third one failed as before but after sleeping](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5197969367236608) :cry:   Since that really sucks, I'm going to do a few more re-runs to see if I can make it happen again...

@mheon sigh...[second occurrence (after 2-3 more re-runs)](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4786868519436288).  So adding the sleep ***did not*** change the behavior substantially, it still [flakes ***AFTER*** also emitting the expected error message](https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/2737/files#diff-763d32052d18ee5114bb2af368392a2bR136) :frowning:

trying with the ``podman pod rm -a`` inside a retry loop.  Perhaps re-running it multiple times will force eventual consistency.  *note:* I don't see this as a fix, just another debugging step.  At this point the race seems likely  to be in ``podman pod rm -a`` (somehow, possibly specific to CI)...
We're getting the error message, but are we printing the ID of the other container? If we found the running pod but not the stopped pod that could explain things.

@mheon Here's [a run where it got 10 retries at the removal and listing](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5704056705122304).  I also added ``pod ps`` printouts for each retry.  The pod ids are the same, and remain consistent for all attempts.  Same for the pod state, they remain `Created` and `Running`, nothing changes :confused: 

I can try sticking the ``--log-level=debug`` back onto the `pod rm -a`, but last time that prevented the race from occurring.  Any other ideas?  (remember, this is reproducing only when running rootless)

There's absolutely no output regarding the other pod. It's not even trying to remove it.
@cevich If we retry, does it always go to 10? Or does the number of failures vary? I have some ideas about the cause now...
@mheon It only took 2 attempts to get it to fail.  My guess is that it always retries 10-times, but I will let you know if I see anything different in a re-run.
Wait a goddamn... I know what this is
Rootless Podman cannot remove multiple pods at the same time. For some reason it's not throwing an error when we try to do this.

How does this test even pass?
@giuseppe we're only joining one pod's userns, but somehow, sometimes, it successfully removes both pods?

[Here's a run with debugging enabled on the rm and a 10-try test failure.](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5665000050917376)

@mheon it ***DOES*** print the 'can't remove pod' error message.  I added a check for this.  Yet still doesn't actually remove the pod.

Re: **How does it pass:**  maybe this is the race, perhaps (somehow) the pod which is suppose to be removed, removes itself coincidentally with the rm command.  That would represent a false-positive condition this test would miss.

@cevich The error message is 100% expected. This test expects one pod to fail.

The issue is that pod 2, the not-running pod, is not even touched. We're not even attempting to remove it.

I think it has to be an ordering thing in the DB - one of the pods will be returned first by the iterator. I think we succeed if the one that's not running is returned first... maybe because we don't need to join its userns because not running?
(Iteration order is based on ID, so there's our randomness - if the pod that is not running has a lower ID, we succeed, else we fail...)

Ohhh okay, I follow.  That explains why retrying the removal fails also.  So is the only thing special in rootless-vs-root, somehow when running as root, the IDs always are the "correct" way around?

(It's possible this problem is happening when run as root, I'm just not seeing it - I also didn't look super closely)
The multiple-pod-removal restriction is rootless only - root pods work as advertised.

Excellent, so it sounds like the problems are fully-known here then.  Whew! What a ride!

Is there anything I can do to help fix this and/or should I just close #2737 then?
Should be fixed by: #2706
Looks like this is fixed.
